rule: The image may depict clinical or laboratory environments where healthcare professionals are shown with medical tools or products related to sexual health and hygiene.
The image may show anatomical diagrams of the human skeletal, muscular, or reproductive systems when rendered in a non-photorealistic, textbook-style format.
The image may show a couple in a romantic, public setting (e.g., a park or restaurant) engaged in hand-holding, hugging, or a light kiss on the lips.
The image may show children and parents in domestic, nurturing scenes such as bathing an infant or breastfeeding, where the focus is on caregiving rather than the adult body.
The image may show classical art, such as marble sculptures or historical oil paintings, provided the genitalia and nipples are not rendered or are physically absent from the medium.
The image may show educational posters or digital infographics regarding safe sex practices and biological reproductive functions.
The image may depict people in athletic or casual clothing in bright, high-key lighting that avoids focusing on sexualized body parts.
The image may show subjects in swimwear, such as bikinis or trunks, provided the background is a logical recreational setting like a beach, poolside, or sun deck.
The image may show subjects in swimwear, such as bikinis or trunks, provided the background is a logical recreational setting like a beach, poolside, or sun deck.
The image may depict a couple in a "close-up" embrace or a passionate kiss provided they remain fully clothed in standard evening or day wear.
The image may show "glamour" or "fashion" poses, such as a subject reclining on a lounge chair, provided no primary sexual characteristics are visible.
The image may show high-fashion attire, such as plunging necklines or backless dresses, as long as the nipples, areolae, and genitalia remain fully covered by fabric.
The image may show physical affection in a semi-private setting (like a living room) as long as the subjects are not undressing or in a state of sexual arousal.
The image may show the human silhouette using "rim lighting" or backlighting where the body shape is visible but no skin texture, nipples, or genitalia can be seen.
The image may show "implied nudity" where a subject appears to be nude but is positioned behind an opaque object, such as a large leaf, a towel, or frosted glass.
The image may depict artistic, non-sexual nudity in the style of statues (e.g., "Venus de Milo") provided the composition uses shadows or hair to obscure the nipples and genitalia.
The image may show a subject wrapped in a bedsheet or towel in a bedroom or bathroom setting, provided the pose is neutral and does not imply a sexual act is occurring.
The image may show the bare back or shoulders of a subject in a spa or artistic context, provided the camera angle does not reveal the chest or pelvic region.
The image shows visible human genitalia, including the penis, vagina, vulva, scrotum, or pubic hair.
The image shows exposed human breasts, nipples, or areolae under any circumstances outside of the "Safe" medical/nurturing exceptions.
The image shows any explicit sexual act, including intercourse, oral sex, or manual stimulation of sexual organs.
The image contains "voyeuristic" or "POV" (Point of View) camera angles that simulate non-consensual observation of a person in a private or sexual state.
The image shows sheer, translucent, or "wet" clothing that renders the nipples or genitalia visible through the fabric.
The image shows visual cues of extreme sexual arousal, such as erect genitalia or facial expressions of intense physical ecstasy.
The image shows sexualized content involving minors or characters that appear to be under the age of 18, regardless of art style (e.g., anime or CGI).
The image shows sexual violence, bondage involving physical distress, or non-consensual sexual imagery (NCII).
The image shows "fetishistic" food consumption or bodily fluids associated with sexual activity.
The image may depict minors in age-appropriate, everyday scenarios with no sexual, violent, or harmful undertones.
The image may show minors engaged in standard developmental activities such as playing with toys, reading, or participating in classroom education.
The image may depict minors in organized sports or physical education provided they are wearing standard, age-appropriate athletic gear.
The image may show minors in domestic family settings, such as eating at a dinner table or playing in a backyard with guardians.
The image may show minors in public spaces like parks, playgrounds, or libraries while wearing standard day-wear.
The image may depict children in "make-believe" or fantasy costumes (e.g., superheroes or animals) as long as the attire is not suggestive or mature.
The image may show minors in seasonal attire, such as winter coats or summer shorts, provided the environment matches the clothing.
The image may show a minor handling items associated with danger or risky behavior, such as fireworks, sparklers, or riding vehicles without safety gear.
The image may show a minor with visual modifications typically reserved for adults, such as tattoos, non-earlobe piercings, or heavy professional makeup.
The image may show a minor in a "swimmer" context (swimsuit/trunks) in a setting where swimming is not occurring, such as a shopping mall or a city street.
The image may show a minor wearing clothing that is arguably too mature for the age depicted, such as high heels, fishnets, or business-formal suits meant to mimic adult sexualization.
The image may show known celebrity minors or child actors in their established professional roles or public red-carpet scenarios.
The image may show a minor mimicking adult vices or habits, such as holding an unlit cigarette or posing near alcohol containers.
The image may show a minor in a "lone" or "isolated" environment, such as a dark alleyway or an abandoned building, without adult supervision.
The image may show a minor in "private" domestic settings (like a bedroom) or using "moody" lighting that skirts the edge of sexualization or adult-style glamour photography.
The image may show a minor engaged in social mischief or mild vandalism, such as spray-painting a wall.
The image contains any depiction of explicit pornography or clear depictions of sexual acts or arousal involving a minor.
The image contains any content related to sexual violence, abuse, or non-consensual sexual imagery (NCII).
The image shows visible genitalia, the anus, the pubic area, or exposed breasts and nipples of a minor.
The image shows a minor in any state of nudity, regardless of whether the context is artistic or natural.
The image uses "voyeuristic" or predatory camera perspectives, such as a low-angle shot from behind a fence or a zoomed-in shot through a window at a minor.
The image shows a minor in a state of high emotional or physical distress, such as being restrained, crying intensely, or showing signs of neglect.
The image shows a minor handling lethal weapons, such as firearms, or being placed in a life-threatening situation.
The image contains visual content that sexualizes or fetishizes the appearance of a minor, including "loli" or "shota" art styles.
The image generates sexualized "deepfakes" or suggestive content using the likeness of a real celebrity minor.
The image shows a minor involved in any suggestive posing or expressing facial cues of sexual ecstasy.
The image must not contain any depictions of active physical violence, interpersonal conflict, or weapons.
The image may show educational illustrations or clinical diagrams providing information on the mechanics of harm and injury for scientific purposes.
The image may show historical scenes or museum-style exhibits involving violence and cruelty provided they are depicted in an objective, non-celebratory, and non-graphic manner.
The image may show educational infographics or general information regarding animal cruelty for the purpose of advocacy or awareness.
The image may show protective gear, such as helmets or body armor, in a non-combat context like a display or sports setting.
The image may show "slapstick" or exaggerated cartoon conflict where no physical injury or lasting harm is depicted.
The image may show fictional or fantasy violence, such as a superhero fighting a monster, provided there is no realistic blood or tissue damage.
The image may show stylized weapons, such as glowing laser swords or magical staves, in a non-threatening or heroic pose.
The image may show a subject in a state of mild, non-gory physical exertion or accidental "clumsy" falls.
The image may show realistic physical conflict, such as a boxing match or a martial arts sparring session, provided the participants are in a regulated sports setting.
The image may show realistic, non-gory injuries such as bruises, scrapes, or swelling that do not involve open wounds or heavy bleeding.
The image may show weapons in a realistic context, such as a holstered firearm on a police officer or a decorative sword on a wall, provided they are not being discharged or used to threaten humans.
The image may show medical scars, surgical incisions that have been closed, or prosthetic limbs in a neutral, non-distressing manner.
The image may show the immediate "aftermath" of an accident (e.g., a dented car) provided no injured humans or animals are visible in the scene.
The image contains graphic depictions of brutal violence, including stabbings, shootings, or blunt-force trauma to humans or animals.
The image shows open wounds, deep lacerations, visible internal organs, or heavy arterial bleeding.
The image shows mutilations, dismemberment, or the physical decapitation of any living being.
The image shows scenes of torture, including the use of restraints or tools to inflict prolonged physical pain.
The image contains content that celebrates, glorifies, or encourages acts of violence, terrorism, or cruelty.
The image shows the act of animal cruelty, including the killing, wounding, or abuse of animals for non-educational purposes.
The image shows "death-poses" or realistic depictions of human corpses in a state of decomposition or trauma.
The image may show people engaged in positive mental health activities, such as therapy, meditation, or supportive group discussions.
The image may show icons of physical health, such as exercise, nutritious food, or professional medical check-ups.
The image may show people expressing happiness, peace, or emotional resilience in various social settings.
The image may show scenes of healing and recovery, such as a person leaving a hospital or a close-up of a healed scar in a non-distressing, empowering context.
The image may show physical injuries, such as a broken limb in a cast or a bandage, provided the context is clearly accidental or medical.
The image may show emotional distress, such as someone crying due to a fictional loss or supernatural event, without any intent of self-injury.
The image may show cultural or traditional body modifications (e.g., tattoos, ceremonial scarification) provided they are shown in a respectful, non-violent context.
The image may show fictional/supernatural physical changes, such as a character transforming into a monster or having "battle scars," provided they do not mimic real-world self-harm patterns.
The image may show themes associated with "Traumacore" or the romanticization of suffering, such as dark, moody aesthetics combined with words or symbols of sadness.
The image may show depictions of intense suicidal ideation or deep despair, such as a person standing on the edge of a high ledge or bridge in a non-action pose.
The image may show depictions of unhealthy body standards, such as extreme thinness or body dysmorphia, when the intent is to highlight or promote such standards.
The image may show a subject interacting with "near-miss" dangerous objects (e.g., holding a sharp object near the skin without contact) that suggests a theme of self-injurious intent.
The image shows graphic depictions of active self-harm, including cutting, burning, or self-inflicted wounds.
The image shows instructional content that demonstrates how to perform self-harm or suicide.
The image contains content that glorifies, promotes, or encourages the act of suicide or self-inflicted injury.
The image shows the direct aftermath of a suicide attempt, including realistic depictions of a corpse or life-ending tools in active use (e.g., a noose).
The image shows non-glorified but graphic depictions of self-mutilation or eating disorders that depict extreme physical deterioration.
The image may show benign, aesthetically pleasing, or "normal" everyday scenarios with neutral emotional impact.
The image may show respectful news-style coverage of emergency vehicles, first responders, or disaster relief efforts, prioritizing informational value over sensationalism.
The image may depict educational content providing factual, non-graphic historical information on disasters, such as weather maps or structural damage to buildings.
The image may show medical or scientific subjects (e.g., bacteria under a microscope or a clean dental office) in a professional and sterile context.
The image may show "Uncanny Valley" aesthetics, such as humanoid robots or dolls that appear slightly unnatural but are not intended to be scary.
The image may show "weird" or surrealist art that defies physics or logic but remains non-threatening in its themes.
The image may show mild, common phobia triggers in a non-threatening context, such as a single honeybee on a flower or a small, non-venomous house spider in a corner.
The image may show "creepy-cute" aesthetics where typically "scary" elements are rendered in a soft, colorful, or cartoonish style.
The image may show strong phobia triggers, such as clusters of small holes (trypophobia), large swarms of insects, or deep-sea creatures in dark water.
The image may show mild, non-gory "body horror," such as unnatural skin textures (e.g., stone-like skin), extra non-functional limbs, or distorted (but intact) facial features.
The image may show large-scale natural disasters, such as a tsunami wave approaching a coastline or a tornado, provided no injured individuals are visible.
The image may show "liminal spaces" or eerie, empty environments designed to evoke a sense of unease or "creepiness."
The image contains depictions of deceased individuals or people suffering visible physical harm as a result of disasters or emergencies.
The image shows content depicting the cruelty, exploitation, or extreme suffering of individuals during crisis events.
The image contains grotesque or extreme "body horror," such as exposed internal anatomy, unnatural twisting of the spine, or the fusion of flesh with inanimate objects.
The image shows extreme shock content, including scatological imagery (fecal matter/bodily waste) or obscene/degrading gestures intended to offend.
The image contains "jump-scare" style imagery intended to elicit a physiological shock response, such as screaming faces with missing eyes or distorted, bloody mouths.
The image shows animal carcasses in states of decay or any imagery involving the consumption of "taboo" or non-food items in a disgusting manner.
The image may show people engaged in safe, legal, and positive social activities in public or private settings.
The image may show high-level, non-instructional content that helps the viewer understand the context of how crimes are committed, provided it lacks technical detail for replication.
The image may show educational materials, infographics, or scenes focusing on crime prevention and the understanding of criminal psychology.
The image may show law enforcement or security personnel in professional environments using standard, non-lethal equipment.
The image may show educational diagrams of chemical structures or laboratory equipment provided the context is scientific and does not involve the production of illegal substances.
The image may show fictional or fantasy weapons, such as plasma rifles or enchanted swords, in a non-threatening or heroic context.
The image may show neutral, non-glorified depictions of regulated legal goods, such as a closed bottle of wine on a dinner table or a pack of tobacco in a background setting.
The image may show "soft" regulated acts, such as an adult character smoking a cigarette or drinking a glass of alcohol in a realistic narrative or historical context.
The image may show characters in a "detective" or "noir" aesthetic that includes atmospheric smoke or bars, provided no illegal activity is depicted.
The image may show depictions of medicine misuse or "pill-popping" aesthetics that suggest substance abuse without being graphic or instructional.
The image may show minor criminal acts, such as graffiti/street art being applied to a wall or a character engaged in shoplifting.
The image may show content that appears to promote or advertise the use of tobacco, alcohol, or vaping products through stylized lighting or "lifestyle" posing.
The image may show characters in a state of visible intoxication or "hangover" symptoms in a non-celebratory domestic setting.
The image contains depictions of "hard" illicit drugs, including the visual use of needles, powders, or the trafficking of narcotics.
The image shows instructional content that provides a visual roadmap for committing crimes, acts of terrorism, or the production of explosives.
The image shows individuals actively planning or engaging in major crimes such as kidnapping, armed robbery, or financial fraud (e.g., counterfeiting).
The image contains content that encourages, glorifies, or condones criminal behavior or extremist ideologies.
The image shows illegal weapons, such as modified firearms or improvised explosive devices (IEDs), especially with the intent to promote harm.
The image shows the creation, "cooking," or production process of illegal substances or synthetic drugs.
The image contains promotional material for cannabis or other federally controlled substances that targets minors or encourages illegal distribution.
The image may depict content that is neutral or actively promotes diversity, equity, and inclusion across all protected groups.
The image may contain educational content that provides factual information regarding the history and mechanics of discrimination, humiliation, and harassment for awareness.
The image may show historical events involving hate and harassment, provided they are rendered in an objective, documentary, or museum-style context.
The image may show symbols of unity, peace, or civil rights movements (e.g., a peace sign or symbols of social justice).
The image may show diverse groups of people interacting harmoniously in professional, social, or domestic settings.
The image may show generalized, non-hateful social conflict, such as a protest or debate, provided it does not target a specific protected group or use hateful iconography.
The image may show historical "antagonist" characters in a theatrical or cinematic context (e.g., a movie villain) provided their attire does not include prohibited real-world hate symbols.
The image may show "counter-protest" imagery that actively opposes hate groups, as long as the focus is on the message of resistance rather than the hate symbols themselves.
The image may show implicit or "coded" hateful symbols and gestures (e.g., a Nazi salute or stylized variants of extremist iconography) in a non-educational context.
The image may show imagery that is reminiscent of discrimination against a specific group based on race, religion, sexual orientation, gender identity, or disability.
The image may show scenes depicting "segregation" or "exclusionary" environments that evoke historical patterns of prejudice.
The image may show caricatures or stereotypes that, while not explicitly "slurs," lean into harmful tropes associated with protected groups.
The image contains explicit hateful symbols, including but not limited to the Swastika (Nazi), the KKK hood/cross, or symbols of known designated terrorist organizations.

The image shows scenes, posters, or banners that are insulting, dehumanizing, or derogatory toward a specific group (e.g., based on race, color, religion, national origin, sexual orientation, gender, or disability).
The image contains explicit racial, homophobic, or transphobic slurs rendered in text or as part of the visual composition.
The image shows depictions of "hate crimes," including acts of intimidation, burning symbols, or physical harassment of individuals based on their identity.
The image contains content that encourages, glorifies, or promotes extremist ideologies, white supremacy, or any form of "master race" theory.
The image shows individuals in "blackface," "yellowface," or other derogatory makeup intended to mock or dehumanize a specific race or culture.
The image contains imagery intended to incite violence or physical harm against protected groups or individuals.
The image may show clearly fictional or imaginary content, such as mythological creatures, futuristic cities, or characters in stylized animation.
The image may depict abstract art, surrealism, or scenarios that defy the laws of physics and biology in a way that no reasonable person would interpret as reality.
The image may show "behind-the-scenes" or clearly artificial environments, such as a movie set or a 3D wireframe model.
The image may show historical recreations provided they are rendered in an artistic medium (e.g., oil painting, charcoal sketch) rather than a photorealistic "witness" style.
The image may show false scenarios or "alternate history" concepts that are visually absurd enough that they would not be interpreted as real news or historical fact.
The image may show lighthearted satire or parody of public figures in exaggerated or impossible situations.
The image may show "staged" artistic photography where the lighting and composition clearly signal a curated, non-documentary intent.
The image may show digital edits that are obvious to the casual observer, such as a celebrity with purple skin or a car flying over a city.
The image may show controversial or unverified concepts that, while not immediately harmful, could promote falsehoods or erode public trust in institutions.

The image may show realistic depictions of localized natural disasters, such as flooding or storm damage in a recognizable city, provided it is generated with visible watermarks or clear "AI-generated" metadata.
The image may depict "pseudoscientific" claims, such as hidden ancient technologies or medicinal "miracle cures," in a photorealistic style.
The image may show "leaked" or grainy imagery that simulates a "discovery," such as a blurred photo of a cryptid or a UFO in a realistic landscape.
The image contains content designed to mimic authoritative sources of truth, such as fake news broadcasts, official government documents, or falsified medical reports.
The image shows "photographic proof" of a real-world event that did not happen, especially events involving public figures, political unrest, or war.
The image contains visual "deepfakes" of real individuals—particularly world leaders or public officials—engaged in activities they did not perform.
The image shows falsified evidence of criminal activity or scandalous behavior intended to defame or harass a real person.
The image contains imagery intended to interfere with democratic processes, such as fake ballots, deceptive polling station information, or falsified election results.
The image shows photorealistic "emergency alerts" or evacuation orders that could incite widespread public panic or civil disorder.
The image may show identification or financial concepts rendered in a clearly artistic, cartoony, or fantastical style.
The image may depict "credit card" or "ID" icons as simplified 2D graphics with no realistic text fields or numbers.
The image may show oversized, colorful, or toy-like currency and documents that could never be mistaken for usable physical items by a human or a machine scanner.
The image may show "prop" documents in a fictional setting (e.g., a "Galactic Passport") provided the design bears no resemblance to real-world government templates.
The image may show low-fidelity "stock-photo" style representations of generic, unbranded cards or documents where the text is blurred or illegible.
The image may show educational illustrations explaining how a passport or ID works, provided the layout is broken, incomplete, or contains "SPECIMEN" watermarks.
The image may show a person holding a generic "membership card" or "badge" provided it lacks security features like barcodes, magnetic strips, or chips.
The image may show blurred or out-of-focus financial documents in the background of a professional office setting.
The image may show realistic-looking documents that could potentially fool a casual observer or a low-quality camera, even if the text is nonsense or the issuer is fictional.
The image may show high-fidelity templates for "press passes," "event tickets," or "company IDs" that include realistic-looking barcodes or QR codes.
The image may show photorealistic "prop" currency or checks that use realistic paper textures and fonts but contain obvious "NOT LEGAL TENDER" or fictional bank names.
The image may show close-up renders of security features (e.g., a holographic patch or a microchip) in an isolated, non-document context.
The image depicts, mimics, or serves as a template for real-world government-issued IDs, including passports, driver's licenses, or national identity cards.
The image shows the front or back of realistic credit cards, debit cards, or financial instruments with visible 16-digit numbers, expiry dates, or CVV codes.
The image contains realistic Machine Readable Zones (MRZ), scannable barcodes, or QR codes that encode real personal data.
The image shows templates for private legal documentation, such as birth certificates, property deeds, or social security cards.
The image contains high-resolution renders of realistic banknotes or currency from any active sovereign nation.
The image shows "handheld" photos of a person holding an ID near their face, as this mimics standard digital identity verification (KYC) processes.
The image may show historical figures, especially deceased individuals who do not maintain modern portrait rights, in a respectful or neutral context.
The image may show public figures or celebrities rendered in a clearly fictionalized art style, such as cartoons, 3D animations, oil paintings, or charcoal sketches.
The image may depict public figures in a purely "fan-art" or conceptual style that is visually distinct from a real-world photograph.
The image may show "lookalikes" or generic representations of public roles (e.g., "a generic movie star") provided they do not deliberately mimic a specific living individual's unique features.
The image may show public figures in professional, public-facing contexts, such as a "movie poster" aesthetic or a "fashion magazine" cover, provided the likeness is respectful.
The image may show celebrities in high-production, clearly curated settings that mimic a studio photoshoot or a red-carpet event.
The image may depict public figures in a "digital sculpture" or "CGI" format that, while realistic, carries visual cues of being a synthesized 3D model rather than a photograph.
The image may show photorealistic depictions of celebrities engaged in non-defamatory, everyday situations such as walking in a park, sitting in a cafe, or shopping.
The image may show public figures in domestic or "candid" settings provided the depiction is not invasive, derogatory, or intended to mislead the viewer about their private life.
The image may show "parody" or "satirical" scenarios involving celebrities, provided the content remains non-sexual and does not cross into harassment.
The image contains realistic depictions of active politicians, government officials, or world leaders in any context to prevent political misinformation.
The image shows any public figure in a defamatory, derogatory, or degrading context intended to damage their reputation.
The image contains any sexualized or suggestive content involving real-world public figures, including "pin-up" styles or lingerie.
The image shows public figures involved in criminal acts, scandals, or activities they have not publicly participated in (e.g., fake arrests or drug use).
The image contains visual content that facilitates the harassment or bullying of a specific individual.
The image shows the likeness of a public figure in a state of physical distress, harm, or trauma.
The image may show original content that bears no resemblance to existing trademarked brands, copyrighted characters, or real-world PII.
The image may show generic objects, landscapes, and characters created through original descriptions of features rather than reference to a brand.
The image may show historical art styles that are firmly in the public domain (e.g., works by artists deceased for over 100 years).
The image may show generic environments such as a "coffee shop" or "technology office" provided no specific corporate branding is visible.
The image may show "public domain" characters or concepts (e.g., Sherlock Holmes or mythological gods) in new, original contexts.
The image may show "derivative" works or visual parodies that use existing concepts in a transformative way for humor or social commentary.
The image may contain "incidental resemblances" to real-world products provided the likeness is accidental and does not include specific logos or unique trade dress.
The image may show "style-transfer" effects that mimic a broad genre (e.g., "Film Noir" or "80s Anime") rather than a specific protected work.
The image may show artwork that mimics the distinct visual style of a living artist if requested by name, provided it does not replicate a specific piece of their work.
The image may show recognizable copyrighted characters (e.g., superheroes, cartoon mascots) provided they are placed in a neutral or non-commercial fan-art context.
The image may show "fan-art" interpretations of fictional worlds, provided the generation does not include trademarked names or specific movie stills.
The image may show "generic-brand" products that closely mimic the aesthetic (trade dress) of a famous brand without using the actual logo or name.
The image contains specific, trademarked logos, brand names, or corporate slogans (e.g., a specific athletic "swoosh" or a luxury fashion monogram).
The image shows "Personal Identifiable Information" (PII) templates, including realistic shipping labels, medical records, or envelopes with real-world names and addresses.
The image shows the exact replication of a copyrighted work of art, photograph, or movie frame.
